title: BPH with Severe Isolated Systolic Hypertension — Which α-Blocker?
subtitle: Resident module: prazosin (non-selective) vs silodosin (uroselective) plus guideline-directed BP control
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Lower urinary tract symptoms with a 40 cc prostate, and severe isolated systolic hypertension
pmh: Benign prostatic enlargement (40 cc — borderline/mildly enlarged) with LUTS
Severe isolated systolic hypertension 170/80 mmHg (wide pulse pressure)
medications: Treatment decision: Option 1 prazosin (Minipress XL) for both, vs Option 2 uroselective α-blocker + guideline-directed antihypertensive

caseDescription: A 67-year-old man has LUTS from a 40 cc (borderline/mildly enlarged) prostate together with severe isolated systolic hypertension (170/80 mmHg, a wide pulse pressure implying rigid vasculature) and CKD stage 3a (eGFR ~55). The teaching question is the choice between Option 1 — a non-selective α1-blocker (prazosin/Minipress XL) used as a single ‘two birds with one stone’ agent for both BPH and hypertension — and Option 2 — a uroselective α1A-blocker (silodosin/Silodal or tamsulosin) for the prostate combined with separate, guideline-directed antihypertensive therapy. Option 2 is preferred: prazosin carries a high risk of first-dose and orthostatic hypotension, syncope, and falls in this rigid-vasculature elderly patient (especially at night with nocturia) and is only a fourth/fifth-line antihypertensive, whereas separating the two targets allows precise, safe titration — a uroselective α-blocker for LUTS plus a long-acting CCB and/or ARB for the blood pressure.
keyFindings: Prostate 40 cc — borderline/mildly enlarged with LUTS
BP 170/80 mmHg — severe isolated systolic hypertension, wide pulse pressure (rigid vasculature → orthostatic risk)
eGFR ~55 (CKD 3a) — favours a renoprotective ARB with early electrolyte/creatinine monitoring
Core decision: prazosin (non-selective α1) for both vs uroselective α-blocker + guideline-directed antihypertensive
finalDiagnosis: BPH/LUTS with severe isolated systolic hypertension and CKD 3a — preferred plan: uroselective α1A-blocker (silodosin, at bedtime) plus guideline-directed antihypertensive (CCB and/or ARB); avoid prazosin as a dual agent
learningObjectives: Explain why a non-selective α1-blocker (prazosin) is unsafe and inadequate to treat both BPH and severe hypertension
Select a uroselective α1A-blocker for LUTS and guideline-directed CCB/ARB therapy for isolated systolic hypertension
Tailor therapy to prostate size, CKD 3a (renoprotection plus monitoring), and orthostatic/fall risk

items.title: Why using prazosin to treat both BPH and hypertension is risky
items.content: It is historically tempting to use a non-selective α1-blocker like prazosin as a single agent for both BPH and hypertension, but modern consensus discourages this because of poor hemodynamic stability. First, severe first-dose and orthostatic hypotension: at 67 with a wide pulse pressure (170/80) implying rigid vasculature, adding a potent systemic vasodilator carries a high risk of orthostatic hypotension, syncope, and falls — especially at night when waking to urinate (nocturia). Second, it is a poor antihypertensive: a BP of 170/80 needs robust, guideline-directed agents (CCBs, ARBs); α1-blockers are strictly fourth- or fifth-line and are notorious for reflex tachycardia and fluid retention that can obscure true BP control.
keyPoints: Rigid vasculature + vasodilator → orthostatic hypotension, syncope, falls (worse with nocturia).
α1-blockers are 4th/5th-line antihypertensives, not primary therapy for 170/80.
They cause reflex tachycardia and fluid retention, obscuring BP control.

items.title: Why a uroselective α1A-blocker (silodosin/tamsulosin) is preferred for the prostate
items.content: Silodosin (Silodal) — and to a lesser degree tamsulosin — selectively targets the α1A-receptors in the prostatic stroma and bladder neck with minimal affinity for the vascular α1B-receptors. This α1A selectivity relieves the LUTS while remaining largely hemodynamically neutral, so it does not produce the unpredictable, precipitous drops in standing blood pressure that prazosin would. Separating the urological target from the blood-pressure target lets each be titrated precisely and safely.
keyPoints: Silodosin is α1A-selective (prostate stroma, bladder neck), sparing vascular α1B.
Relieves LUTS with near hemodynamic neutrality.
Avoids the precipitous standing-BP drops of prazosin.

items.title: How to treat the isolated systolic hypertension (170/80)
items.content: Bring the systolic pressure down with primary, long-acting agents, not an α-blocker. A long-acting calcium channel blocker (e.g. amlodipine 5–10 mg) is highly effective for isolated systolic hypertension in the elderly, lowering pressure by arterial vasodilation without the profound orthostatic risk of non-selective α-blockers. An ARB or ACE inhibitor (e.g. telmisartan 40–80 mg) gives excellent 24-hour control and endothelial protection; given his eGFR ~55 (CKD stage 3a), an ARB also provides important long-term renoprotection — provided serum potassium and creatinine are checked within 1–2 weeks of starting. These are the foundational classes that safely smooth out a 170 mmHg systolic baseline.
keyPoints: Long-acting CCB (amlodipine) — excellent for elderly isolated systolic hypertension.
ARB/ACEi (telmisartan) — 24-h control plus renoprotection in CKD 3a.
Check potassium and creatinine within 1–2 weeks of starting an ARB/ACEi.

items.title: How prostate size (40 cc) guides therapy
items.content: At 40 cc the prostate is transitioning from normal to enlarged. If his symptoms are mild to moderate, a uroselective α-blocker is the correct initial symptomatic step and is usually sufficient. A 5-α-reductase inhibitor (e.g. dutasteride or finasteride) is added later only if there is progressive mechanical obstruction, a clearly enlarged gland, or a rising PSA — because 5-ARIs shrink the gland over months and are most useful in larger prostates. So the sequence here is: uroselective α-blocker first; reserve the 5-ARI for documented progression or significant gland enlargement.
keyPoints: 40 cc is borderline — α-blocker first for mild/moderate symptoms.
Add a 5-α-reductase inhibitor only for progressive obstruction, larger gland, or rising PSA.
5-ARIs act over months and suit larger prostates.

items.title: The summary recommendation and execution pearl
items.content: Verdict: Option 1 (prazosin/Minipress XL as a dual agent) is not recommended — high risk of orthostatic syncope, poor systolic control, and falls. Option 2 (uroselective α-blocker plus a standard antihypertensive) is preferred — uroselective LUTS relief combined with predictable, guideline-directed BP control. Execution pearl: start the uroselective α-blocker (silodosin) strictly at bedtime to further blunt any minor twilight dizziness, and pair it with a foundational agent such as amlodipine or telmisartan to safely lower the 170 mmHg systolic baseline. Treat the two problems with two precise tools, not one blunt one.
keyPoints: Option 1 (prazosin for both): not recommended.
Option 2 (silodosin + CCB/ARB): preferred.
Pearl: silodosin at bedtime; pair with amlodipine or telmisartan.

items.question: Why are α1-blockers such as prazosin only fourth- or fifth-line antihypertensives?
items.answer: Because they control blood pressure inconsistently and carry disproportionate risk. As systemic vasodilators they cause first-dose and orthostatic hypotension — dangerous in older patients with rigid arteries — and they provoke reflex tachycardia and sodium/fluid retention that blunt and obscure their own antihypertensive effect. Outcome trials have not shown them to be as protective as the primary classes (CCBs, ARBs/ACE inhibitors, thiazide-type diuretics), and one large trial signalled worse heart-failure outcomes with an α-blocker. So they are reserved as add-on therapy (often where there is concurrent BPH), not used as foundational blood-pressure treatment.
keyPoints: Systemic vasodilation → first-dose/orthostatic hypotension.
Reflex tachycardia and fluid retention undercut BP control.
Weaker outcome evidence than the primary classes → add-on, not first-line.

items.question: How does α1A-selectivity make silodosin hemodynamically safer than prazosin?
items.answer: The α1-adrenoceptors that tighten the prostatic stroma and bladder neck are predominantly the α1A subtype, whereas the receptors that maintain vascular tone are predominantly α1B. Silodosin binds α1A with high selectivity and has minimal affinity for α1B, so it relaxes the bladder-neck/prostate smooth muscle (relieving obstruction) without significantly dilating blood vessels. Prazosin, being non-selective, blocks vascular α1B as well, producing the precipitous standing blood-pressure falls and syncope risk. Targeting the right subtype is what separates LUTS relief from systemic hypotension.
keyPoints: Prostate/bladder-neck tone = α1A; vascular tone = α1B.
Silodosin is α1A-selective, sparing vascular α1B → hemodynamic neutrality.
Non-selective prazosin blocks α1B too, causing orthostatic hypotension.

items.question: Design the optimal initial regimen for this 67-year-old with a 40 cc prostate, BP 170/80, and eGFR 55, and state one key monitoring step.
items.answer: For the LUTS: a uroselective α1A-blocker — silodosin (or tamsulosin) — started at bedtime to minimise any twilight dizziness; reserve a 5-α-reductase inhibitor (e.g. dutasteride) for later if there is progressive obstruction, a larger gland, or rising PSA. For the isolated systolic hypertension: a long-acting CCB (amlodipine 5–10 mg) and/or an ARB (telmisartan 40–80 mg); the ARB is particularly valuable for renoprotection given CKD 3a. Avoid prazosin as a dual agent. Key monitoring step: check serum potassium and creatinine within 1–2 weeks of starting the ARB/ACE inhibitor, and assess for orthostatic symptoms.

items.stem: For this 67-year-old with BPH (40 cc) and severe isolated systolic hypertension (170/80), what is the best overall strategy?
options: Prazosin (Minipress XL) as a single agent to treat both conditions
A uroselective α1A-blocker (e.g. silodosin) for the LUTS plus a guideline-directed antihypertensive (CCB and/or ARB) for the blood pressure
An ARB alone, ignoring the LUTS
No treatment until the prostate enlarges further
items.explanation: Separating the two targets is safest and most effective: a uroselective α-blocker relieves LUTS with hemodynamic neutrality, while a CCB and/or ARB provides robust, guideline-directed control of the systolic hypertension. Using prazosin for both risks orthostatic syncope and controls the BP poorly.

items.stem: Why is prazosin particularly hazardous in this patient?
options: It causes severe hyperkalemia
Its systemic α1 blockade in a rigid-vasculature elderly man risks first-dose/orthostatic hypotension, syncope, and falls — especially with nocturia
It is nephrotoxic at standard doses
It interacts dangerously with all CCBs
items.explanation: A wide pulse pressure signals stiff arteries; adding a non-selective systemic vasodilator like prazosin can precipitate orthostatic hypotension and syncope, with a high fall risk on waking to urinate at night. It is not primarily hyperkalemic or nephrotoxic.

items.stem: Which agent is a uroselective α1A-blocker that relieves LUTS with minimal effect on blood pressure?
options: Prazosin
Doxazosin
Silodosin
Phenoxybenzamine
items.explanation: Silodosin is highly α1A-selective (prostate stroma and bladder neck) with minimal vascular α1B affinity, giving hemodynamic neutrality. Prazosin and doxazosin are non-selective with greater orthostatic risk, and phenoxybenzamine is a non-selective irreversible α-blocker used for pheochromocytoma.

items.stem: Which antihypertensive approach is most appropriate for this elderly man with isolated systolic hypertension and CKD 3a?
options: An α1-blocker as first-line therapy
A long-acting CCB (amlodipine) and/or an ARB (telmisartan), the ARB adding renoprotection
A short-acting nifedipine capsule as needed
No pharmacologic therapy; lifestyle only for 170/80
items.explanation: Long-acting CCBs are excellent for elderly isolated systolic hypertension, and an ARB gives 24-hour control plus renoprotection valuable in CKD 3a. α-blockers are not first-line, short-acting nifedipine is unsafe, and 170/80 warrants pharmacologic treatment.

items.stem: After starting telmisartan (an ARB) in this patient with eGFR ~55, what monitoring is essential?
options: Liver function tests at 6 months only
Serum potassium and creatinine within 1–2 weeks of initiation
Daily PSA measurement
No monitoring is needed
items.explanation: RAAS blockade can raise potassium and transiently reduce eGFR, so serum potassium and creatinine should be checked within 1–2 weeks of starting (or up-titrating) an ARB/ACE inhibitor, particularly in CKD.

items.stem: When would a 5-α-reductase inhibitor (e.g. dutasteride) be appropriate to add for this 40 cc prostate?
options: Immediately, as mandatory first-line therapy
Only if there is progressive mechanical obstruction, a clearly enlarged gland, or a rising PSA
Never — it is contraindicated in BPH
Instead of any α-blocker for acute symptom relief
items.explanation: At 40 cc with mild/moderate symptoms a uroselective α-blocker is the correct first step. A 5-ARI, which shrinks the gland over months, is added later for progressive obstruction, a larger gland, or rising PSA — not as the initial agent or for rapid relief.

items.stem: To minimise twilight dizziness, when should the uroselective α-blocker (silodosin) be taken?
options: First thing in the morning fasting
At bedtime
With each meal three times daily
Only on alternate days
items.explanation: Dosing the uroselective α-blocker at bedtime helps blunt any minor orthostatic or twilight dizziness, improving tolerability while the foundational antihypertensive controls the systolic pressure.

items.statement: Non-selective α1-blockers such as prazosin are recommended first-line antihypertensives.
items.explanation: False. α1-blockers are fourth/fifth-line antihypertensives; the primary classes are CCBs, ARBs/ACE inhibitors, and thiazide-type diuretics.

items.statement: Silodosin is α1A-selective, sparing vascular α1B receptors and giving relative hemodynamic neutrality.
items.explanation: True. Its α1A selectivity targets the prostate/bladder-neck while largely sparing vascular α1B, so it relieves LUTS without the orthostatic drops of non-selective agents.

items.statement: Long-acting calcium channel blockers such as amlodipine are effective for isolated systolic hypertension in elderly patients.
items.explanation: True. Long-acting CCBs lower systolic pressure via arterial vasodilation and are well suited to elderly isolated systolic hypertension.

items.statement: An ARB offers renoprotection in CKD stage 3a but requires monitoring of potassium and creatinine after initiation.
items.explanation: True. ARBs (and ACE inhibitors) provide renoprotection but can raise potassium and transiently reduce eGFR, so electrolytes and creatinine are checked within 1–2 weeks of starting.

items.statement: At 40 cc with mild symptoms, a 5-α-reductase inhibitor is the mandatory first-line therapy.
items.explanation: False. A uroselective α-blocker is the correct initial step for mild/moderate LUTS at this size; a 5-ARI is reserved for progressive obstruction, a larger gland, or rising PSA.

items.statement: Using prazosin to treat both BPH and hypertension increases the risk of orthostatic syncope and falls.
items.explanation: True. Its systemic vasodilation, especially in a rigid-vasculature elderly patient with nocturia, raises the risk of orthostatic hypotension, syncope, and falls.

items.assertion: A uroselective α1A-blocker such as silodosin is preferred over prazosin for this patient’s LUTS.
items.reason: Its α1A selectivity relieves LUTS while sparing vascular α1B receptors, avoiding the precipitous orthostatic blood-pressure drops prazosin would cause.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Because the obstructive tone is α1A-mediated and silodosin spares vascular α1B, it treats the LUTS without the systemic hypotension that makes non-selective prazosin hazardous here.
